stem: Suppose \( \lambda = [[lam]] \) is an eigenvalue of a matrix \( A \). What is the corresponding eigenvalue of the matrix \( A^{2} + ([[k]])A + ([[m]])I \)?
solution: If \( \lambda \) is an eigenvalue of \( A \), then \( p(\lambda) \) is the corresponding eigenvalue of \( p(A) \). Here \( p(\lambda) = \lambda^{2} + ([[k]])\lambda + ([[m]]) = ([[lam]])^{2} + ([[k]])([[lam]]) + ([[m]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the linear term \( k\lambda \).
This drops the constant term \( m \).
This uses the wrong sign on the linear term.
This differentiates \( p \) instead of evaluating it at \( \lambda \).
This replaces \( \lambda^{2} \) with \( \lambda \), forgetting to square.
This is one more than the correct eigenvalue.
This is two less than the correct eigenvalue.

stem: Suppose \( \lambda = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \) is an eigenvalue of an invertible matrix \( A \). What is the corresponding eigenvalue of \( A^{-1} \)?
solution: If \( \lambda \) is an eigenvalue of an invertible \( A \), the corresponding eigenvalue of \( A^{-1} \) is \( \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{[[q]]}{[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This negates \( \lambda \) instead of taking its reciprocal.
This returns \( \lambda \) itself, the eigenvalue of \( A \), not of \( A^{-1} \).
This squares \( \lambda \) instead of inverting it.
This takes the reciprocal but with the wrong sign.
This is one more than the correct eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct eigenvalue.

stem: Suppose \( \lambda = [[lam]] \) is an eigenvalue of a matrix \( A \), and \( A - ([[c]])I \) is invertible. What is the corresponding eigenvalue of \( \left(A - ([[c]])I\right)^{-1} \)?
solution: \( A - ([[c]])I \) has eigenvalue \( \lambda - [[c]] = [[lam]] - [[c]] \), so its inverse has eigenvalue \( \dfrac{1}{[[lam]] - [[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the eigenvalue of \( A - cI \) itself, forgetting to take the reciprocal for the inverse.
This has the sign of the shift reversed inside the reciprocal.
This uses \( \lambda \) alone, ignoring the shift by \( c \).
This adds \( c \) instead of subtracting it before inverting.
This is one more than the correct eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct eigenvalue.

stem: Suppose \( \lambda = [[lam]] \) is an eigenvalue of a matrix \( A \). What is the corresponding eigenvalue of \( A^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: If \( \lambda \) is an eigenvalue of \( A \), the corresponding eigenvalue of \( A^{n} \) is \( \lambda^{n} \). Here that is \( ([[lam]])^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( \lambda \) by \( n \) instead of raising it to the \( n \)-th power.
This returns \( \lambda \) itself, the eigenvalue of \( A \), not of \( A^{n} \).
This is off by one in the exponent, using \( n-1 \) instead of \( n \).
This is off by one in the exponent, using \( n+1 \) instead of \( n \).
This is one more than the correct eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct eigenvalue.

stem: A \( 3 \times 3 \) invertible matrix \( A \) has eigenvalues \( [[l1]] \), \( [[l2]] \), and \( [[l3]] \). Since \( \operatorname{adj}(A) = \det(A)\,A^{-1} \), the eigenvalue of the adjugate \( \operatorname{adj}(A) \) corresponding to the eigenvalue \( [[l1]] \) of \( A \) is the product of the OTHER two eigenvalues. What is that eigenvalue of \( \operatorname{adj}(A) \)?
solution: Since \( \operatorname{adj}(A) = \det(A) A^{-1} \), the eigenvalue of \( \operatorname{adj}(A) \) paired with \( [[l1]] \) is \( \det(A)/[[l1]] = \dfrac{[[l1]][[l2]][[l3]]}{[[l1]]} = [[l2]]\cdot[[l3]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \det(A) \) itself, not the eigenvalue of the adjugate.
This multiplies \( [[l1]] \) with only one of the other eigenvalues instead of using the two OTHER eigenvalues.
This multiplies \( [[l1]] \) with only one of the other eigenvalues instead of using the two OTHER eigenvalues.
This adds the two other eigenvalues instead of multiplying them.
This is one more than the correct eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct eigenvalue.
